enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based product roadmap and product intelligence platform that helps product managers plan, visualize, and communicate strategy across teams.
enrichment.pricing_model: Seat-based subscription billed annually. Pricing is per active editor while viewers who only need to see a shared roadmap are unlimited and free on every plan. Enterprise pricing is custom and quote-based.
enrichment.starting_price: From $39 per editor per month (Basic plan, billed annually)
enrichment.company_size: Estimated 51-100 employees (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.headquarters: Denver, Colorado, United States (originally founded in Santa Barbara, California)
enrichment.seo_title: ProductPlan Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what ProductPlan offers in 2026: roadmap software pricing, key features, pros and cons, and how it compares for product management teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProductPlan is a cloud-based product roadmap and product intelligence platform founded in 2013 by Jim Semick and Greg Goodman in Santa Barbara, California. The company operated for years without outside venture capital, building a customer base that includes organizations such as Nike, Intuit, NASA, Autodesk, and PBS. ProductPlan is now headquartered in Denver, Colorado, and states it has helped teams create more than 860,000 roadmaps and execute over 23 million strategic initiatives. In 2025, Denver-based private equity firm Bow River Capital acquired a majority stake in ProductPlan, and in 2026 ProductPlan acquired the AI startup Winware.ai, using that acquisition as the foundation for a new Product Intelligence Platform under CEO Steven Cohn, a four-time founder who previously built Winware.ai. The core product lets product managers build drag-and-drop visual roadmaps, timeline and swimlane views, and portfolio views for organizations running multiple roadmaps at once, along with prioritization boards that tie initiatives to strategic goals such as OKRs. The newer Product Intelligence layer adds idea capture, research surveys, and video-call feedback aggregation so customer input can be validated and linked directly to roadmap items, with AI-suggested initiatives surfacing patterns across collected feedback. ProductPlan integrates with tools such as Jira, Azure DevOps, Slack, and Salesforce, and includes risk and dependency tracking plus launch management for coordinating go-to-market timing. Administrative features include single sign-on, advanced admin controls, and white-glove onboarding with dedicated customer success support on higher tiers. Pricing is seat-based and billed annually: organizations only pay for editors who build and manage roadmaps, while viewers are unlimited and included at no extra cost on every plan. Published pricing lists a Basic plan starting around $39 per editor per month and a Professional plan in the $69 to $79 per editor per month range, with Enterprise pricing quoted individually for larger deployments; ProductPlan does not publish a free-forever tier but offers a 14-day free trial. On G2, ProductPlan holds a 4.3 out of 5 rating from more than 200 reviews, with reviewers frequently praising its visual clarity, ease of building executive-ready roadmaps, and drag-and-drop editing, while some reviews note that costs can add up for larger teams of editors and that deeper customization or reporting can require higher-tier plans. The product is aimed primarily at product managers, product marketing teams, and executives who need to align cross-functional stakeholders around a shared strategic plan rather than a granular engineering backlog; teams that need detailed sprint-level task tracking typically pair ProductPlan with a separate delivery tool such as Jira. As of 2026, ProductPlan competes most directly with Productboard, Aha!, and Roadmunk in the dedicated product-roadmap software category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProductPlan is a purpose-built roadmapping platform for product managers who need to turn strategy into a visual, shareable plan. Founded in 2013 and now headquartered in Denver, Colorado, the company has spent over a decade focused specifically on the roadmapping category, rather than trying to be a full project management or engineering ticketing system.

Since its 2025 majority acquisition by Bow River Capital and its 2026 acquisition of Winware.ai, ProductPlan has pushed beyond static roadmap visuals into a broader Product Intelligence Platform that connects customer feedback, research, and AI-suggested initiatives directly to the roadmap itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, ProductPlan offers drag-and-drop roadmap building, timeline and swimlane views, and portfolio views that let leadership see multiple team roadmaps in one place. Prioritization boards allow teams to score and rank initiatives, and dedicated OKR functionality ties day-to-day roadmap items back to company-level objectives.

Newer Product Intelligence features include idea capture forms, research surveys, and the ability to pull feedback straight from recorded video calls, all of which feed into an opportunity-validation workflow. Risk and dependency tracking, launch management, and integrations with Jira, Azure DevOps, Slack, and Salesforce round out the platform for teams coordinating roadmap work with delivery teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProductPlan uses seat-based, annually billed pricing where only editors (people who build and manage roadmaps) count toward the seat total; viewers who are shared a roadmap link are unlimited and free on every plan. This structure is meant to encourage broad internal visibility without penalizing companies for sharing roadmaps widely.

Published plans start with a Basic tier around $39 per editor per month, a Professional tier in the $69 to $79 per editor per month range, and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier for larger organizations that need SSO, advanced admin controls, and dedicated onboarding. There is no permanent free plan, but a 14-day free trial is available.

features.title: Drag-and-drop roadmap builder
features.description: Build timeline, swimlane, and Gantt-style roadmap views without spreadsheets, and rearrange initiatives visually as priorities shift.

features.title: Portfolio views
features.description: Roll up multiple team or product-line roadmaps into a single portfolio view for leadership visibility across the organization.

features.title: Prioritization boards
features.description: Score and rank initiatives against custom criteria so teams can defend roadmap decisions with data rather than opinion.

features.title: OKR integration
features.description: Link roadmap initiatives directly to company objectives and key results to show how day-to-day work ladders up to strategy.

features.title: Product Intelligence feedback capture
features.description: Collect ideas, run research surveys, and pull insights from recorded customer calls, then validate opportunities before committing roadmap space.

features.title: AI-suggested initiatives
features.description: Surface patterns across captured feedback and research to recommend candidate roadmap initiatives automatically.

features.title: Risk, dependency, and launch management
features.description: Track cross-team dependencies and coordinate go-to-market launch timing alongside the roadmap itself.

features.title: Enterprise administration
features.description: Single sign-on, advanced admin permissions, and white-glove onboarding are available for larger deployments.

pros: Purpose-built for roadmapping, with a cleaner and faster learning curve than general-purpose project tools repurposed for roadmaps
Unlimited free viewers on every plan makes it easy to share roadmaps broadly across an organization
Strong visual polish that produces executive-ready roadmap presentations with minimal formatting work
Solid third-party integrations with Jira, Azure DevOps, Slack, and Salesforce for connecting roadmaps to delivery work
cons: No permanent free tier, only a 14-day trial, so evaluating long-term fit requires a time-boxed test
Seat-based editor pricing can get expensive as more product managers and stakeholders need edit access
Not a replacement for sprint-level task tracking, so many teams still need a separate delivery tool alongside it
Recent ownership changes (2025 majority acquisition, 2026 Winware.ai acquisition) mean the product roadmap and pricing structure could continue to evolve

faqs.question: What is ProductPlan used for
faqs.answer: ProductPlan is used by product managers and product teams to build, visualize, and share strategic product roadmaps, prioritize initiatives, and align stakeholders around company objectives.

faqs.question: How much does ProductPlan cost
faqs.answer: ProductPlan's published Basic plan starts around $39 per editor per month billed annually, with a Professional plan in the $69 to $79 per editor per month range and custom Enterprise pricing for larger teams. Viewers are unlimited and free on every plan.

faqs.question: Does ProductPlan offer a free plan
faqs.answer: ProductPlan does not offer a permanent free plan, but it does offer a 14-day free trial so teams can evaluate the product before committing.

faqs.question: Who owns ProductPlan
faqs.answer: ProductPlan was founded in 2013 by Jim Semick and Greg Goodman. In 2025, Denver-based private equity firm Bow River Capital acquired a majority stake in the company.

faqs.question: Is ProductPlan good for engineering task management
faqs.answer: No. ProductPlan is focused on strategic roadmapping rather than sprint-level task or ticket management, so most engineering teams pair it with a tool like Jira for day-to-day delivery work.

faqs.question: What is ProductPlan's Product Intelligence Platform
faqs.answer: Launched in 2026 following the acquisition of AI startup Winware.ai, the Product Intelligence Platform adds feedback capture, research surveys, and AI-suggested initiatives on top of ProductPlan's existing roadmapping tools.

faqs.question: What integrations does ProductPlan support
faqs.answer: ProductPlan integrates with tools including Jira, Azure DevOps, Slack, and Salesforce, among others, to connect roadmap planning with delivery and CRM systems.

faqs.question: What is ProductPlan's G2 rating
faqs.answer: ProductPlan holds a 4.3 out of 5 rating on G2 based on more than 200 reviews, with users highlighting its visual clarity and ease of use.

use_cases.title: Cross-functional roadmap alignment
use_cases.description: Product leaders build a single visual roadmap that engineering, sales, marketing, and executives can all view without needing edit access.
use_cases.audience: Product managers and VPs of Product at mid-size to enterprise software companies

use_cases.title: Portfolio-level planning
use_cases.description: Organizations running multiple products or teams use portfolio views to compare and coordinate roadmaps across the whole product line.
use_cases.audience: Heads of Product and product operations teams managing several product lines

use_cases.title: Customer feedback-driven prioritization
use_cases.description: Teams capture ideas and research directly in the platform and use AI-suggested initiatives to translate validated customer feedback into prioritized roadmap items.
use_cases.audience: Product managers focused on discovery and customer-driven roadmap prioritization

enrichment.short_description: Free subscription analytics tool that automatically calculates MRR, ARR, churn, and other SaaS metrics, now offered as ProfitWell Metrics inside Paddle's billing and payments platform.
enrichment.pricing_model: ProfitWell Metrics itself is free to use indefinitely. Paddle, the parent company that acquired ProfitWell, charges merchants a percentage-based fee on revenue processed through its merchant-of-record billing platform.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (ProfitWell Metrics); Paddle's billing platform fees are transaction-based and quoted per merchant
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 395-400 employees (Paddle, the parent company, as of 2026)
enrichment.headquarters: Originally Boston, Massachusetts, United States; now operated by Paddle, headquartered in London, United Kingdom
enrichment.seo_title: ProfitWell Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Paddle Acquisition Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what happened to ProfitWell after its 2022 acquisition by Paddle, how ProfitWell Metrics works today, and whether it still fits your SaaS analytics stack.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProfitWell was founded in 2012 in Boston by Patrick Campbell, initially operating as a pricing strategy consultancy called Price Intelligently before evolving into a broader subscription-analytics company. Campbell bootstrapped the business for roughly a decade without outside funding, cashing out his 401k to cover initial living expenses, and grew it into a suite of products serving SaaS and subscription businesses: Price Intelligently for pricing strategy and optimization, Retain for automated churn reduction, and ProfitWell Metrics, a free tool that automatically calculates MRR, ARR, churn, LTV, and other core subscription metrics by connecting directly to a company's billing data. In May 2022, payments infrastructure company Paddle acquired ProfitWell for more than 200 million dollars in cash and equity, funded in part by Paddle's own 200 million dollar Series D raise. It was Paddle's first acquisition. As part of the deal, ProfitWell CEO Patrick Campbell became Paddle's Chief Strategy Officer while ProfitWell's chief product officer Facundo Chamut retained his product leadership role, and ProfitWell continued operating under its own brand while the two companies integrated their products through the rest of 2022. As of 2026, ProfitWell Metrics remains available and free to use, rebranded in Paddle's own materials as ProfitWell Metrics by Paddle, and still takes only a few minutes to connect to a company's existing payments stack. It continues to support integrations with Stripe, Chargebee, Braintree, and other billing systems, though Paddle has clearly prioritized the deepest ongoing feature development for merchants who use Paddle Billing as their core payments and subscription-management platform. In practice, ProfitWell today functions less as a standalone company and more as a free analytics and lead-generation layer that sits on top of Paddle's paid merchant-of-record billing product, which handles global payments, tax compliance, subscription billing, and checkout for software companies. Paddle itself is headquartered in London, with roughly 395 to 400 employees globally as of 2026, and has raised over 290 million dollars in funding at a valuation of around 1.4 billion dollars from investors including KKR, FTV Capital, and BGF. For a directory or comparison context, it is important to note that ProfitWell is no longer an independently operated, separately priced SaaS product; the ProfitWell brand and its free Metrics tool now function as an entry point into the Paddle ecosystem, and businesses evaluating it for retention or pricing-optimization capabilities should expect deeper integration with, and eventual migration toward, Paddle's core billing platform rather than a fully separate roadmap.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProfitWell began in 2012 as Price Intelligently, a Boston-based pricing consultancy founded by Patrick Campbell, before evolving into a full subscription-analytics company known for its free MRR, ARR, and churn-tracking product. After roughly a decade of bootstrapped growth, Paddle acquired ProfitWell in May 2022 for more than 200 million dollars.

Today, ProfitWell operates as ProfitWell Metrics within the Paddle ecosystem. The free metrics dashboard remains available on its own, but the deepest product investment and integration now flows through Paddle's broader payments and subscription billing platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ProfitWell Metrics connects to a company's existing billing data (Stripe, Chargebee, Braintree, Paddle Billing, and others) and automatically calculates core subscription metrics including MRR, ARR, churn rate, LTV, and customer counts, without requiring manual spreadsheet work.

Historically, the broader ProfitWell suite also included Retain, an automated churn-reduction tool for recovering failed payments, and Price Intelligently, a pricing strategy service; both capabilities have been folded into Paddle's product and services offering since the acquisition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProfitWell Metrics itself remains free to use, with setup that Paddle describes as taking only a few minutes once a billing integration is connected. There is no paid tier for the core metrics dashboard.

Because ProfitWell is now part of Paddle, the meaningful pricing consideration for most businesses is Paddle's own merchant-of-record billing platform, which charges a percentage-based fee on transactions it processes rather than a flat subscription price.

features.title: Automated MRR and ARR tracking
features.description: Automatically calculates monthly and annual recurring revenue directly from connected billing data.

features.title: Churn and retention reporting
features.description: Tracks customer and revenue churn over time to help SaaS teams spot retention problems early.

features.title: Customer lifetime value (LTV) calculation
features.description: Estimates LTV automatically based on historical subscription and payment data.

features.title: Multi-processor billing integrations
features.description: Connects with Stripe, Chargebee, Braintree, Paddle Billing, and other payment processors.

features.title: Benchmarking data
features.description: Historically provided benchmark comparisons against other SaaS companies using aggregated, anonymized ProfitWell data.

features.title: Retain churn-recovery tooling
features.description: Automated dunning and failed-payment recovery capability, now offered as part of Paddle's broader product and services suite.

pros: Core metrics dashboard remains genuinely free with no credit card required to start
Quick setup that connects directly to existing billing and payment data
Backed by Paddle's larger, well-funded payments infrastructure and engineering team
Useful benchmarking heritage from years of aggregated SaaS metrics data
cons: No longer an independently operated product roadmap; direction is now set by Paddle's priorities
Deepest ongoing feature development is increasingly oriented toward companies using Paddle Billing specifically
Some previously distinct ProfitWell products (Retain, Price Intelligently) have been absorbed into Paddle's broader offering rather than sold as standalone tools
Companies not using or considering Paddle as their billing provider may find long-term support and roadmap commitment less certain

faqs.question: Is ProfitWell still a separate company
faqs.answer: No. Paddle acquired ProfitWell in May 2022 for more than 200 million dollars, and ProfitWell now operates as ProfitWell Metrics within the Paddle product ecosystem.

faqs.question: Is ProfitWell Metrics still free
faqs.answer: Yes, ProfitWell Metrics remains free to use as of 2026, automatically calculating MRR, ARR, churn, and LTV from a company's connected billing data.

faqs.question: What happened to ProfitWell's Retain and Price Intelligently products
faqs.answer: Retain (churn reduction) and Price Intelligently (pricing strategy) were part of the original ProfitWell suite and have been integrated into Paddle's broader product and services offering since the 2022 acquisition.

faqs.question: Does ProfitWell Metrics work with Stripe
faqs.answer: Yes, ProfitWell Metrics integrates with Stripe as well as Chargebee, Braintree, Paddle Billing, and other common billing and payment processors.

faqs.question: Who founded ProfitWell
faqs.answer: ProfitWell was founded in 2012 by Patrick Campbell in Boston, originally as a pricing consultancy called Price Intelligently.

faqs.question: What is Patrick Campbell's role today
faqs.answer: After the acquisition, ProfitWell founder and CEO Patrick Campbell became Paddle's Chief Strategy Officer.

faqs.question: How much did Paddle pay for ProfitWell
faqs.answer: Paddle acquired ProfitWell in May 2022 for more than 200 million dollars in cash and equity, funded in part by Paddle's 200 million dollar Series D round.

faqs.question: Should I use ProfitWell if I do not use Paddle for billing
faqs.answer: ProfitWell Metrics still integrates with non-Paddle processors like Stripe, Chargebee, and Braintree, so it can be used independently of Paddle Billing, though Paddle's newest features are generally built first for its own billing customers.

use_cases.audience: Founders and finance teams at subscription businesses that want free, automated recurring-revenue reporting

use_cases.description: Revenue and customer-success teams use ProfitWell's metrics and historical Retain capabilities to monitor and reduce involuntary and voluntary churn.
use_cases.audience: Customer success and RevOps teams at subscription companies

use_cases.title: Evaluating Paddle as a billing provider
use_cases.description: Companies exploring merchant-of-record billing test ProfitWell Metrics as a free entry point before adopting Paddle's full payments and subscription platform.
use_cases.audience: SaaS companies evaluating a switch to a merchant-of-record billing provider

features: Integrations with Stripe, Chargebee, Braintree, and Paddle Billing

pricing_plans.price: Percentage-based transaction fee, quoted per merchant
features: Access to ProfitWell Metrics and retention tooling

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted file sharing software that lets businesses securely send and receive files with clients through their own server.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source under the GNU GPL v2 license. There are no license fees; the only cost is the hosting infrastructure a user chooses to run it on.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); users only pay for their own server or hosting, which can start at just a few dollars per month
enrichment.company_size: Open-source community project maintained by volunteer contributors; not a commercial company with a disclosed headcount
enrichment.headquarters: No corporate headquarters (open-source project); originally created by developer Emmanuel B. Muriaga
enrichment.seo_title: ProjectSend Review 2026: Free Self-Hosted File Sharing Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore ProjectSend, the free open-source self-hosted file sharing tool: features, setup, pricing, pros and cons, and how it compares to paid alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProjectSend is a free, open-source, self-hosted file sharing application built specifically for businesses that need to exchange files securely with clients rather than the general public. The project traces back to 2012 and was originally created by developer Emmanuel B. Muriaga; it is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 2, meaning the source code is freely available, modifiable, and redistributable, and there are no license fees for using it. Because ProjectSend is self-hosted, organizations install it on their own server, VPS, or shared hosting account (typically running PHP and MySQL/MariaDB), which means the only ongoing cost is whatever hosting infrastructure the organization chooses; budget VPS providers advertise hosting suitable for ProjectSend for just a few dollars per month. The core workflow centers on client relationships: administrators create client accounts or client groups, then upload files that are shared privately with a specific client or group, and optionally allow clients to upload files back into a designated folder. This two-way exchange, combined with role-based permissions for internal staff users, makes it popular with agencies, accountants, consultants, web developers, and any business that regularly exchanges sensitive documents with external clients and wants to avoid relying on third-party cloud storage services. Beyond the core upload and download workflow, ProjectSend includes detailed activity logging that tracks actions taken by both staff users and clients, download tracking, automatic expiration for uploads, email notifications, support for S3-compatible object storage as a backend, and the ability to generate public file links when needed. The interface supports more than 70 languages through community-contributed translations available via Transifex, making it usable for organizations operating outside English-speaking markets. Administrators can customize branding, including logos and color themes, so the portal can be presented to clients as part of the company's own tooling rather than an obviously third-party product. The software is optimized for mobile browsers as well as desktop use. Because it is self-hosted and open source, ProjectSend appeals most to technically capable teams or IT departments that want full control over where client files live, without recurring per-user SaaS fees; the tradeoff is that the organization is responsible for its own hosting, backups, security patching, and uptime, unlike a managed SaaS file-sharing product. Development continues through the projectsend GitHub organization, with releases and translation updates published on projectsend.org. For directory and comparison purposes, ProjectSend competes with self-hosted alternatives like Nextcloud and FileRun, as well as commercial client-portal products like ShareFile and Content Snare, differentiating itself primarily through being fully free, open source, and purpose-built around client file exchange rather than general team collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProjectSend is a free, open-source PHP application that businesses install on their own server to share and receive files with clients securely. It dates back to 2012 and was originally created by developer Emmanuel B. Muriaga, and it is released under the GNU GPL v2 license, meaning anyone can use, inspect, and modify the code at no cost.

Unlike consumer cloud storage tools, ProjectSend is purpose-built around client relationships: administrators create client accounts or groups and control exactly which files each client can see, download, or upload, all from infrastructure the organization fully controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include client and client-group management, role-based permissions for internal staff, two-way file exchange (clients can be allowed to upload as well as download), detailed activity logging, download tracking, and automatic upload expiration.

The platform also supports S3-compatible object storage as a backend, custom branding and themes so the portal can match a company's own look and feel, mobile-optimized browsing, and an interface translated into more than 70 languages through community contributions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProjectSend itself carries no license or subscription fee; it is entirely free and open source under GPL v2. There is no tiered pricing, feature gating, or user-count limit built into the software itself.

The only real cost is hosting: since ProjectSend is self-hosted, an organization needs a server or VPS capable of running PHP and a MySQL/MariaDB database, with basic hosting for small deployments available from various providers for just a few dollars per month.

features.title: Client and client-group accounts
features.description: Create individual client logins or group clients together to control exactly who can see which shared files.

features.title: Two-way file exchange
features.description: Allow clients not just to download files but also to upload files back into a folder assigned specifically to them.

features.title: Role-based staff permissions
features.description: Assign different access levels to internal team members so not everyone has full administrative control.

features.title: Activity logging and download tracking
features.description: Every upload, download, and administrative action is logged, giving a full audit trail of file activity.

features.title: Automatic upload expiration
features.description: Set files to automatically expire after a defined period, useful for time-sensitive document sharing.

features.title: S3-compatible storage support
features.description: Store files on S3-compatible object storage instead of, or alongside, local server storage.

features.title: Custom branding and themes
features.description: White-label the client portal with your own logo and color scheme so it feels like part of your own product.

features.title: 70-plus language translations
features.description: The interface is available in more than 70 languages thanks to community-contributed translations.

pros: Completely free and open source with no per-user or per-feature license costs
Full control over data since files are stored on infrastructure the organization owns
Purpose-built two-way client file exchange rather than generic team file storage
Extensive language support makes it usable well beyond English-speaking markets
cons: Requires self-hosting, so the organization is responsible for server setup, security patching, and backups
No official managed cloud version, unlike SaaS competitors that handle infrastructure for you
Interface and feature set are more utilitarian than modern SaaS client-portal products
Support relies on community documentation and forums rather than a dedicated commercial support team

faqs.question: Is ProjectSend really free
faqs.answer: Yes, ProjectSend is completely free and open source under the GNU GPL v2 license, with no license fees. The only cost is the hosting infrastructure you run it on.

faqs.question: Do I need technical skills to install ProjectSend
faqs.answer: Yes, some technical knowledge is helpful since ProjectSend is self-hosted and typically requires a server running PHP and a MySQL or MariaDB database, though many hosting providers offer one-click installers.

faqs.question: Can clients upload files, not just download them
faqs.answer: Yes, ProjectSend supports two-way file exchange, so administrators can allow specific clients to upload files into a folder assigned only to them.

faqs.question: How many languages does ProjectSend support
faqs.answer: ProjectSend's interface is available in more than 70 languages through community-contributed translations distributed via Transifex.

faqs.question: Who created ProjectSend
faqs.answer: ProjectSend was originally created by developer Emmanuel B. Muriaga, with the project dating back to 2012 and continuing today as an open-source community project.

faqs.question: Does ProjectSend support cloud storage backends
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to local server storage, ProjectSend supports S3-compatible object storage.

faqs.question: Is ProjectSend a good alternative to Dropbox or Google Drive for client file sharing
faqs.answer: For businesses that want a self-hosted, client-focused portal with full data control and no per-user fees, ProjectSend is a common open-source alternative, though it lacks the polish and real-time collaboration features of consumer cloud storage tools.

faqs.question: Can I customize ProjectSend's branding
faqs.answer: Yes, ProjectSend supports custom branding and themes so the client portal can be presented under your own company's look and feel.

use_cases.title: Agency and consultancy client file exchange
use_cases.description: Agencies and consultants use ProjectSend to securely send deliverables to clients and receive source files or feedback documents back, all under their own branding.
use_cases.audience: Marketing agencies, design studios, and independent consultants

use_cases.title: Accounting and professional services document sharing
use_cases.description: Firms that handle sensitive financial or legal documents use ProjectSend's self-hosted model to keep client files on infrastructure they control rather than third-party cloud storage.
use_cases.audience: Accounting firms, law firms, and other regulated professional services

use_cases.title: Internal IT-managed client portal
use_cases.description: IT departments deploy ProjectSend as a lightweight, no-license-fee client portal integrated into existing server infrastructure and backup processes.
use_cases.audience: IT teams at small and mid-size businesses that prefer self-hosted software

features: Full source code and unlimited installs

enrichment.short_description: Open-source systems monitoring and alerting toolkit built around a time-series database and the PromQL query language, widely used for cloud-native and Kubernetes observability.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source under the Apache License 2.0. Prometheus itself has no vendor pricing; many companies offer paid managed-Prometheus or Prometheus-compatible hosting as a separate commercial layer on top of the open-source project.
enrichment.company_size: Not a company; an open-source project governed by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) under the Linux Foundation, with dozens of active maintainers and 1,000+ contributors
enrichment.headquarters: No corporate headquarters. The project originated at SoundCloud in Berlin, Germany, and its governing foundation, CNCF, is headquartered in San Francisco, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Prometheus Monitoring Review 2026: Features, Architecture and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Understand what Prometheus is, how its architecture and PromQL query language work, its pricing model, and how it compares to other monitoring tools in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Prometheus is an open-source systems monitoring and alerting toolkit that originated at SoundCloud in 2012, built by engineers who needed a more scalable and reliable way to monitor their infrastructure than existing tools provided at the time. In May 2016, Prometheus became the second project accepted into the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), joining shortly after Kubernetes, and in August 2018 it became the second CNCF project to reach graduated status, the foundation's highest maturity designation, signaling that the project had demonstrated thriving adoption, a documented and structured governance process, and a strong commitment to community sustainability. Prometheus is released under the Apache License 2.0 and is entirely free to use, modify, and redistribute; there is no company that owns or sells Prometheus itself, though a large ecosystem of vendors offers managed or Prometheus-compatible hosted services (such as Grafana Cloud and various cloud providers' managed Prometheus offerings) as a commercial layer on top of the open-source core. Architecturally, Prometheus uses a pull-based model: a central Prometheus server scrapes metrics at regular intervals from configured targets, rather than requiring applications to push data to it, which makes it well suited to dynamic, containerized environments like Kubernetes where instances are constantly created and destroyed. Metrics are stored in a purpose-built, efficient time-series database, and users query and aggregate that data using PromQL, Prometheus's own functional query language that supports filtering, aggregation, and mathematical operations across time series. The broader Prometheus ecosystem includes client libraries for instrumenting application code directly, and exporters, which are small adapter services that translate metrics from third-party systems (databases like MySQL, PostgreSQL and Redis, web servers like NGINX, and general Linux/Unix hosts via node_exporter) into the Prometheus metrics format. Alerting is handled by a separate component called Alertmanager, which receives alerts fired by evaluated rules on the Prometheus server and manages deduplication, grouping of related alerts, routing to the correct team or notification channel, and silencing of known issues. By the time of its CNCF graduation, Prometheus already had roughly 20 active maintainers, more than 1,000 contributors, and over 13,000 commits, and both the contributor base and production usage have continued to grow substantially since, with Prometheus now considered a de facto standard for metrics-based monitoring in cloud-native and Kubernetes environments, frequently paired with Grafana for visualization. Because it is free, self-hosted by default, and has no formal SLA or vendor support included, organizations adopting Prometheus at scale often either build internal expertise to operate and scale it themselves or pay for a managed/hosted Prometheus-compatible service from a commercial vendor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Prometheus is an open-source monitoring system and time-series database that originated at SoundCloud in 2012 to solve internal infrastructure-monitoring needs, and was later contributed to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) in 2016 as its second hosted project after Kubernetes. It graduated to CNCF's highest maturity tier in 2018.

There is no company behind Prometheus in the traditional sense; it is maintained by a large open-source community under CNCF governance, and released under the permissive Apache License 2.0, making it free to use, self-host, and modify for any purpose.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Prometheus uses a pull-based scraping model, where the Prometheus server actively collects metrics from configured targets at regular intervals, which fits naturally with dynamic, containerized infrastructure such as Kubernetes clusters. Collected data is stored in an efficient built-in time-series database and queried using PromQL, Prometheus's own powerful query language for filtering and aggregating metrics.

The ecosystem includes client libraries for instrumenting custom application code, and a large catalog of exporters that translate metrics from existing systems like databases, web servers, and operating systems into Prometheus's format. A separate Alertmanager component handles deduplication, grouping, and routing of fired alerts to the right notification channels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Prometheus itself is completely free, with no licensing cost, usage limits, or paid tiers, since it is open-source software released under the Apache License 2.0.

Organizations that want a managed experience rather than operating Prometheus themselves can pay third-party vendors for managed or Prometheus-compatible hosted monitoring services, but that is a separate commercial layer, not something Prometheus or CNCF sells directly.

features.title: Pull-based metrics scraping
features.description: The Prometheus server actively pulls metrics from configured targets on a schedule, well suited to dynamic and containerized infrastructure.

features.title: Built-in time-series database
features.description: Metrics are stored efficiently in a purpose-built local time-series database optimized for monitoring workloads.

features.title: PromQL query language
features.description: A powerful functional query language for filtering, aggregating, and performing calculations across collected time-series data.

features.title: Client libraries
features.description: Official libraries let developers instrument their own application code to expose custom metrics directly to Prometheus.

features.title: Exporter ecosystem
features.description: A large catalog of exporters translates metrics from third-party systems, such as databases, web servers, and hosts, into Prometheus format.

features.title: Alertmanager
features.description: A dedicated component handles deduplication, grouping, routing, and silencing of alerts fired from Prometheus rules.

features.title: Kubernetes-native service discovery
features.description: Prometheus can automatically discover scrape targets in dynamic environments like Kubernetes without manual configuration.

features.title: CNCF-graduated governance
features.description: As a graduated CNCF project, Prometheus follows documented governance and maturity standards with broad community and vendor backing.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing costs or vendor lock-in
De facto standard for Kubernetes and cloud-native monitoring with a massive ecosystem of exporters and integrations
Powerful, purpose-built PromQL query language for flexible metrics analysis
Backed by CNCF graduated-project governance, meaning long-term project stability and broad community support
cons: Requires self-hosting and operational expertise to run and scale reliably at production volume
Local storage is not designed for very long-term retention without additional remote-storage or federation setup
No official vendor support or SLA included, since it is a community-governed open-source project
Learning PromQL and designing effective alerting rules has a real learning curve for teams new to metrics-based monitoring

faqs.question: Is Prometheus free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Prometheus is completely free and open source, released under the Apache License 2.0, with no licensing fees or usage limits.

faqs.question: Who created Prometheus
faqs.answer: Prometheus originated at SoundCloud in 2012 to address internal infrastructure-monitoring needs, and was later contributed to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation in 2016.

faqs.question: Is Prometheus a CNCF graduated project
faqs.answer: Yes, Prometheus became the second project to reach CNCF's graduated status in August 2018, following Kubernetes.

faqs.question: What query language does Prometheus use
faqs.answer: Prometheus uses PromQL, its own functional query language designed for filtering, aggregating, and performing calculations on time-series metrics data.

faqs.question: Does Prometheus handle alerting
faqs.answer: Prometheus evaluates alerting rules and fires alerts, and a separate component called Alertmanager handles deduplication, grouping, routing, and silencing of those alerts.

faqs.question: Is Prometheus good for Kubernetes monitoring
faqs.answer: Yes, Prometheus's pull-based scraping and native service discovery make it one of the most widely used monitoring systems for Kubernetes and other cloud-native environments.

faqs.question: Does Prometheus require a company or license to use commercially
faqs.answer: No, Prometheus can be used, modified, and deployed commercially at no cost under the Apache License 2.0, since it has no owning company or commercial license requirement.

faqs.question: What are Prometheus exporters
faqs.answer: Exporters are small adapter applications that translate metrics from third-party systems, like MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis, or NGINX, into the Prometheus metrics format so they can be scraped and monitored.

use_cases.title: Kubernetes and container monitoring
use_cases.description: Platform and SRE teams use Prometheus to automatically discover and monitor dynamic Kubernetes workloads, pods, and nodes at scale.
use_cases.audience: SRE and platform engineering teams running containerized infrastructure

use_cases.title: Application performance metrics
use_cases.description: Development teams instrument their own services with Prometheus client libraries to expose custom business and performance metrics for monitoring.
use_cases.audience: Backend engineering teams building instrumented, observable services

use_cases.title: Infrastructure and alerting pipelines
use_cases.description: Operations teams pair Prometheus with Alertmanager and Grafana to build end-to-end infrastructure monitoring and on-call alerting pipelines.
use_cases.audience: DevOps and infrastructure teams building self-hosted observability stacks

enrichment.seo_title: PromptLayer Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how PromptLayer helps AI teams manage, evaluate, and monitor prompts and agents in production, including pricing, features, and pros and cons for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: PromptLayer is an LLM engineering platform founded in 2021 by Jonathan Pedoeem and Jared Zoneraich and headquartered in New York City. The company builds tooling aimed at the growing gap between prototyping with large language models and running LLM-powered features reliably in production, focusing specifically on prompt management, versioning, evaluation, and observability for both simple prompt-based applications and more complex AI agents. In February 2025, PromptLayer announced a 5 million dollar seed funding round led by Ivan Bercovich at ScOp VC and Peter Boyce II at Stellation Capital, with participation from Ben Lang, Thales Partners, Verissimo Ventures, and a group of notable angel investors including Joshua Browder (CEO of DoNotPay), Romain Huet (Head of Developer Experience at OpenAI), and Logan Kilpatrick (Product Lead for Google AI Studio), signaling strong credibility within the AI tooling ecosystem. The core product centers on a prompt registry that treats prompts as versioned, deployable artifacts rather than hardcoded strings buried in application code: teams get immutable version history, commit messages, diffs between versions, and the ability to label and route specific versions to production without redeploying application code. A visual, no-code prompt editor allows non-engineering stakeholders, such as domain experts, product managers, or subject-matter specialists, to propose and test prompt changes and run side-by-side comparisons or A/B tests without needing a developer in the loop for every iteration. On the evaluation side, PromptLayer supports running prompts against historical usage data, comparing outputs across different models, scheduling recurring regression tests to catch quality drift, and running one-off batch evaluation jobs. As AI applications have shifted from single prompts toward multi-step agents, PromptLayer has extended its versioning and tracing capabilities to agents specifically, automatically tracking changes to agent configurations over time so teams can experiment safely while keeping a known-good production version stable, along with tracing that surfaces usage volume and cost data across an agent's execution steps. PromptLayer's pricing follows a freemium model: a permanent free tier is aimed at individual developers, offering a capped number of monthly requests, limited log retention, and modest storage; a Pro plan starts at 49 dollars per month for small teams; a Team plan around 500 dollars per month adds a hard cap of roughly 25 users; and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing along with compliance features such as HIPAA and SOC 2 support, role-based access control, webhooks, and flexible deployment options including self-hosting on GCP, AWS, or Azure, or single-tenant and EU-hosted cloud deployments for regulated or data-residency-sensitive customers. As a smaller, venture-backed company operating in the fast-moving LLM tooling space, PromptLayer is best suited to engineering and product teams that want dedicated prompt and agent version control plus observability, rather than a full end-to-end LLM application framework.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PromptLayer is a New York-based LLM engineering platform founded in 2021 by Jonathan Pedoeem and Jared Zoneraich, built to help engineering and product teams manage prompts and AI agents the way software teams manage code: with versioning, testing, and observability rather than hardcoded strings scattered across an application.

The company raised a 5 million dollar seed round in February 2025 from investors including ScOp VC, Stellation Capital, and notable AI-industry angels, and has positioned itself as a registry-first platform focused specifically on making prompt and agent changes auditable, testable, and safely deployable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of the platform is a prompt registry with immutable version history, commit messages, and diffs, plus the ability to label a specific version for production use without redeploying application code. A visual, no-code editor lets non-technical stakeholders propose and test prompt changes directly.

PromptLayer also supports evaluation workflows (comparing models, scheduling regression tests, running batch evaluations against historical data), tracing with usage and cost visibility, and dedicated agent versioning that tracks configuration changes over time so teams can experiment without destabilizing production agents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: PromptLayer offers a permanent free tier for individual developers with a capped monthly request volume, limited log retention, and modest storage, alongside a Pro plan starting at 49 dollars per month for small teams.

A Team plan priced around 500 dollars per month adds a roughly 25-user cap, while Enterprise customers get custom pricing with compliance certifications, role-based access control, and flexible deployment options including self-hosting on major cloud providers or single-tenant and EU-hosted cloud environments.

features.title: Prompt registry with version control
features.description: Store prompts as versioned, deployable artifacts with commit messages and diffs, similar to how source code is managed.

features.title: Visual no-code prompt editor
features.description: Let non-engineering stakeholders edit, test, and compare prompt variations without developer involvement for every change.

features.title: Model comparison and A/B testing
features.description: Run the same prompt against multiple models or prompt variants to compare quality, cost, and latency side by side.

features.title: Evaluation and regression testing
features.description: Schedule recurring regression tests and run batch evaluations against historical usage data to catch quality drift before it reaches users.

features.title: Agent versioning
features.description: Automatically track configuration changes to AI agents over time, keeping a stable production version while enabling safe experimentation.

features.title: Tracing and cost visibility
features.description: Trace requests through prompts and agents with visibility into usage volume and cost at each step.

features.title: Enterprise compliance and self-hosting
features.description: HIPAA, SOC 2, GDPR, and CCPA support, along with self-hosted deployment on GCP, AWS, or Azure for regulated or data-sensitive customers.

features.title: Role-based access control and webhooks
features.description: Enterprise-tier access controls and webhook events for integrating prompt and agent lifecycle events into other systems.

pros: Dedicated, registry-first approach to prompt versioning that is stronger than general-purpose LLM frameworks bolted onto observability
Permanent free tier makes it accessible for individual developers and small projects to start immediately
No-code visual editor genuinely opens prompt iteration up to non-engineering team members
Enterprise-grade compliance and self-hosting options exist for regulated industries despite being a relatively small company
cons: Free tier's request and storage caps are fairly modest for anything beyond light individual use
Team plan's roughly 25-user cap can force fast-growing teams into custom Enterprise pricing sooner than expected
As a smaller, newer company, long-term roadmap stability is less proven than larger, more established observability vendors
Primarily focused on prompts and agent versioning rather than being a full end-to-end LLM application development framework

faqs.question: What does PromptLayer do
faqs.answer: PromptLayer is an LLM engineering platform for managing, versioning, evaluating, and monitoring prompts and AI agents used in production applications.

faqs.question: Does PromptLayer have a free plan
faqs.answer: Yes, PromptLayer offers a permanent free tier for individual developers with a capped monthly request volume, 7-day log retention, and limited storage.

faqs.question: How much does PromptLayer cost
faqs.answer: PromptLayer's Pro plan starts at 49 dollars per month, with a Team plan around 500 dollars per month and custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Who founded PromptLayer
faqs.answer: PromptLayer was founded in 2021 by Jonathan Pedoeem and Jared Zoneraich and is headquartered in New York City.

faqs.question: Can non-technical team members use PromptLayer
faqs.answer: Yes, PromptLayer includes a visual, no-code prompt editor designed to let non-engineering stakeholders propose and test prompt changes directly.

faqs.question: Does PromptLayer support AI agents, not just prompts
faqs.answer: Yes, PromptLayer extends its versioning and tracing capabilities to AI agents, automatically tracking configuration changes over time.

faqs.question: Does PromptLayer offer self-hosting
faqs.answer: Yes, Enterprise customers can choose self-hosted deployment on GCP, AWS, or Azure, or single-tenant and EU-hosted cloud options.

faqs.question: How much funding has PromptLayer raised
faqs.answer: PromptLayer announced a 5 million dollar seed funding round in February 2025 led by ScOp VC and Stellation Capital, with participation from several notable AI-industry angel investors.

enrichment.pricing_model: Flat-rate subscription, not per-user. Two plans give unlimited users access at a fixed monthly price, billed monthly or annually.

enrichment.seo_title: ProofHub Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how ProofHub's flat-rate project management and collaboration software works in 2026, including pricing plans, key features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProofHub is an all-in-one project management and team collaboration platform founded in 2012 by Sandeep Kashyap, who continues to serve as CEO. The company is headquartered in Covina, California, with a significant operations office in Chandigarh, India, and has grown as a bootstrapped, privately held business; according to reported figures, ProofHub reached roughly 6.5 million dollars in annual revenue and around 85,000 customers by 2024, with an employee count that industry sources estimate in the 30 to 45 range. ProofHub's core differentiator in the crowded project management category is its flat-rate pricing model: rather than charging per user like most competitors (Asana, monday.com, ClickUp, and others), ProofHub charges a single fixed monthly price that includes unlimited users, which can make it significantly more cost-effective for larger teams, agencies, or organizations that need to include external clients and contractors without paying incremental per-seat fees. The platform combines task management with support for multiple views including Kanban boards, Gantt charts, and calendar views, along with built-in time tracking, custom workflows and statuses, and dedicated online proofing tools that let teams review, mark up, and approve creative files like images, PDFs, and documents directly within the platform, a feature that is particularly popular with marketing and creative agencies. Other core modules include group chat and discussions for team communication without needing a separate messaging tool, file storage and sharing, and built-in reporting on project and resource status. ProofHub currently offers two pricing tiers: an Essential plan at a flat 45 dollars per month that includes access to 40 projects, unlimited users, and 15 GB of storage, and an Ultimate Control plan that unlocks unlimited projects, more storage, and advanced workflow customization, priced with an introductory rate around 89 dollars per month for new customers before increasing to a standard rate closer to 150 dollars per month; both plans include unlimited users at no additional per-seat cost. ProofHub does not require long-term contracts, allows cancellation at any time, and offers a free trial with unlimited projects and no credit card required so prospective customers can evaluate the full feature set before committing. The product is positioned primarily for small to mid-size businesses, marketing and creative agencies, and any organization that regularly collaborates with external clients or contractors and wants predictable costs regardless of how many people need access, rather than large enterprises needing deep custom integrations or complex portfolio management across many product lines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProofHub is a project management and team collaboration platform founded in 2012 by Sandeep Kashyap, who still leads the company as CEO today. Headquartered in Covina, California, with a significant operations presence in Chandigarh, India, ProofHub has grown as a privately held, largely bootstrapped business serving tens of thousands of customers.

What sets ProofHub apart from most competitors in the crowded project management category is its flat-rate pricing: instead of charging per user, ProofHub charges one fixed monthly fee that covers unlimited team members, clients, and contractors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ProofHub combines task management across multiple views (including Kanban boards, Gantt-style timelines, and calendars) with time tracking, custom workflows and statuses, and built-in group chat and discussions, so many teams can avoid paying for separate messaging or time-tracking tools.

A standout feature is ProofHub's online proofing tool, which lets teams review, annotate, and approve creative files like images, PDFs, and documents directly within the platform, a workflow that is especially valuable for marketing and creative agencies managing client approvals.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProofHub offers two flat-rate plans, both including unlimited users: the Essential plan at 45 dollars per month (40 projects, 15 GB storage), and the Ultimate Control plan, which unlocks unlimited projects and more storage, priced from around 89 dollars per month for new customers before increasing to a standard rate near 150 dollars per month.

Because pricing is flat rather than per seat, ProofHub can be substantially less expensive than per-user competitors for larger teams or organizations that need to include many external collaborators. A free trial with unlimited projects and no credit card requirement is available before committing to a paid plan.

faqs.question: How is ProofHub priced
faqs.answer: ProofHub uses flat-rate pricing rather than per-user pricing. The Essential plan is 45 dollars per month and the Ultimate Control plan is priced from around 89 dollars per month initially rising to about 150 dollars per month, both including unlimited users.

faqs.question: Does ProofHub charge per user
faqs.answer: No, ProofHub is one of the few project management tools that charges a single flat monthly fee covering unlimited users rather than charging per seat.

faqs.question: Who founded ProofHub
faqs.answer: ProofHub was founded in 2012 by Sandeep Kashyap, who remains the company's CEO.

faqs.question: Does ProofHub offer a free trial

faqs.question: What is ProofHub's proofing feature
faqs.answer: ProofHub includes a built-in online proofing tool that lets teams review, mark up, and approve images, PDFs, and other files directly within the platform, popular with creative and marketing teams.

faqs.question: Where is ProofHub headquartered
faqs.answer: ProofHub is headquartered in Covina, California, with a significant operations office in Chandigarh, India.

faqs.question: Is ProofHub good for agencies
faqs.answer: Yes, ProofHub's flat-rate pricing and built-in proofing tools make it a popular choice for marketing and creative agencies that need to include many external clients without extra per-seat costs.

faqs.question: Can I cancel ProofHub anytime
faqs.answer: Yes, ProofHub does not require long-term contracts and can be cancelled at any time.
